title: Känguru der Mathematik 2025 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2025_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: Pablo has six balloons. He gives away two of his balloons. How many balloons does Pablo have now?
questions.solution: Pablo starts with 6 balloons and gives away 2. 6 − 2 = 4 balloons left.
solution_steps: Start: 6 balloons.
Give away 2.

questions.question_text: How many of these shapes are triangles?
questions.solution: Count only the shapes with exactly 3 straight sides. There are 6 triangles.
solution_steps: Look at each shape one by one.
A triangle has exactly 3 straight sides.
Counting only those gives 6.

questions.question_text: How many pencils are in this picture?
questions.solution: Follow each pencil from tip to end and count carefully. There are 8 pencils.
solution_steps: Trace each pencil from its point to its end.
Count so none is missed or doubled.
Total = 8.

questions.question_text: Felix forms a square out of 16 small grey tiles. He then removes some of the small tiles (see picture). How many small tiles did he remove?
questions.solution: A full square needs 16 tiles. 10 grey tiles are still there, so 16 − 10 = 6 were removed.
solution_steps: A full square = 16 small tiles.
Count the grey tiles still in the picture: 10.
16 − 10 = 6 removed.

questions.question_text: Five baby kangaroos jump along the marked lines to their mothers. Which path is the shortest?
questions.solution: Every path covers the same left-to-right distance, so only the up-and-down parts differ. Path A has the fewest extra up/down steps, so it is the shortest.
solution_steps: All paths start and finish at the same columns.
Their horizontal length is the same.
The one with the least up/down zig-zag is shortest → A.

questions.question_text: Gala glues together 18 cubes. Then she stretches two rubber bands around them (see picture). How many cubes are NOT touched by any of the rubber bands?
questions.solution: Each rubber band only presses on the outer cubes it passes over. The cubes hidden away from both bands number 6.
solution_steps: 18 cubes are glued into a block.
A band touches only the cubes it runs across.
The cubes neither band reaches number 6.

questions.solution: Compare the cut-out pattern of each key with the keyhole. Only key D matches the lock.
solution_steps: Look at the shape of the keyhole.
Check each key's teeth/cut-outs.
Key D is the matching one.

questions.question_text: A dog has 2 puppies that both weigh the same. Picture 1 shows the dog and one puppy weigh 13 kilograms. Picture 2 shows the dog and both puppies weigh 16 kilograms. How many kilograms does the big dog weigh?
questions.solution: Adding the second puppy raised the total from 13 kg to 16 kg, so one puppy = 16 − 13 = 3 kg. The dog = 13 − 3 = 10 kg.
solution_steps: Dog + 1 puppy = 13 kg.
Dog + 2 puppies = 16 kg.
Extra puppy = 16 − 13 = 3 kg.

questions.question_text: Jan puts 12 pieces of fruit on a table. Vera takes away 2 pears, 4 apples and half of the oranges. Now there are only oranges left. How many oranges are left?
questions.solution: The 2 pears and 4 apples (6 fruits) are removed, leaving 6 oranges. Vera takes half of them, so 6 ÷ 2 = 3 oranges remain.
solution_steps: 12 fruits in all.
Remove 2 pears + 4 apples = 6, leaving 6 oranges.
Half the oranges are taken: 6 ÷ 2 = 3 left.

questions.question_text: All the buttons on Carola's sweater look exactly the same (shown at the top). Which one of these buttons is from her sweater?
questions.solution: Carola's button has a particular hole pattern. Comparing each choice, only button E has exactly the same pattern.
solution_steps: Note the hole pattern on Carola's button.
Check the holes on each option.
Button E matches.

questions.question_text: Alex threads white and black beads alternately onto a string. Twice, 5 beads are hidden (see picture). How many white beads are there in total?
questions.solution: The beads go white, black, white, black… Filling the two hidden groups of 5 while keeping the alternating pattern gives 6 white beads in total.
solution_steps: The colours alternate white/black along the string.
Fill the two hidden runs of 5 keeping that pattern.
Counting the white beads gives 6.

questions.question_text: 17 squirrels are sitting on 4 trees. There are at least 2 squirrels on each tree. The number of squirrels is different on each tree. What is the largest possible number of squirrels on one tree?
questions.solution: To leave the most for one tree, the other three hold the smallest different amounts: 2, 3 and 4 = 9. So the biggest tree has 17 − 9 = 8 squirrels.
solution_steps: Each tree: a different number, at least 2.
Smallest three different amounts: 2 + 3 + 4 = 9.
17 − 9 = 8 on the last tree.

questions.question_text: Mike has these 2 building blocks. Which of the objects CANNOT be built with these two blocks?
questions.solution: Picture combining Mike's two blocks for each object. Objects A–D can each be made, but object E cannot be built from those two blocks.
solution_steps: Imagine fitting Mike's two blocks together.
Test each object shape.
Only E cannot be built.

questions.question_text: Every time a coin is put into the machine, the bottom ball falls out of one of the five tubes. How many coins does Barbara have to put in to be sure she gets at least one white ball?
questions.solution: In the unluckiest case every coloured (non-white) ball drops first. After all of those are gone, the next coin must give a white ball — that takes 11 coins to be certain.
solution_steps: Imagine the worst order: all non-white balls come out first.
Count those non-white balls.
One more coin then guarantees a white ball → 11.

questions.question_text: Amir has stickers with different ladybugs. They have 1, 2, 3 or 4 dots on their wings. Amir fills the grid so that there are ladybugs with 1, 2, 3 and 4 dots in every row and in every column. What should the top row look like when Amir is finished?
questions.solution: Each row and column must contain 1, 2, 3 and 4 dots once each, like a mini Sudoku. Using the ladybugs already placed, the top row must be the arrangement shown in option B.
solution_steps: Every row and column needs 1, 2, 3, 4 dots exactly once.
Use the ladybugs already in the grid to avoid repeats.
The only top row that fits is option B.
